title: Reeb Orbits Frequently Intersecting a Symplectic Surface
speaker: Michael Hutchings
abstract: Consider a contact three-manifold, and within it a symplectic surface with boundary on Reeb orbits. We show that assuming a certain inequality on the rotation numbers of the boundary Reeb orbits, there must exist Reeb orbits which intersect the interior of the surface. Moreover, we obtain such orbits with an explicit lower bound on the “frequency” of these intersections (the number of intersections divided by symplectic action).  Applying this result to the special case of a global surface of section leads to a generalization of various recent results relating the mean action to the Calabi invariant for area-preserving surface diffeomorphisms.

proof_stages.label: Contact and symplectic-surface setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a closed contact three-manifold with Reeb flow, an embedded compact surface whose interior is transverse to the Reeb vector field and whose boundary lies on Reeb orbits, and defines the intersection frequency of a periodic Reeb orbit as intersection number divided by action.

proof_stages.label: Area-over-volume benchmark and non-generic obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Generic equidistribution of Reeb orbits implies, by thickening the surface along the flow, that some orbit sequence has intersection frequency tending to area(Σ)/vol(Y), but non-generic contact forms can fail to have interior-intersecting orbits, so the desired statement must be weakened.

proof_stages.label: Main non-generic frequency theorem with boundary orbits
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem asserts that the supremum over periodic Reeb orbits, including suitably interpreted boundary orbits, of the intersection frequency is at least area(Σ)/vol(Y), with the boundary contribution defined via nearby trajectories or equivalently the linearized rotation relative to the surface.

proof_stages.label: Calabi/mean-action application via open books
proof_stages.summary: For a Hamiltonian or zero-flux area-preserving surface diffeomorphism with boundary rotation θ0, the speaker defines the action function, Calabi invariant, and mean action, then obtains an infimum mean-action bound by realizing the map as the return map of a Reeb flow on an open book and applying the main theorem to its page.

proof_stages.label: Elementary spectral-invariant machinery
proof_stages.summary: The proof uses elementary contact spectral invariants c_k, defined through holomorphic curves with point constraints, together with spectrality, a monotonicity/capacity inequality for conformal changes of the contact form, and a Weyl law relating asymptotics of c_k to contact volume.

proof_stages.label: Perturbation near the surface and spectral upper bound
proof_stages.summary: Assuming no orbit has sufficiently high intersection frequency, the contact form is increased on a small flow-thickening of Σ, and spectrality shows that the derivative of the relevant spectral invariants along this deformation is small because all spectral orbit sets intersect the thickening too infrequently.

proof_stages.label: Capacity/Weyl contradiction and limiting argument
proof_stages.summary: The monotonicity/capacity inequality and Weyl asymptotics give a lower bound on the same spectral change, contradicting the upper bound and yielding quantitative action bounds, which then allow passage from nondegenerate approximations to degenerate contact forms.

examples.name: Global surface of section as an allowed surface
examples.note: A global surface of section is mentioned only to make concrete that the permitted symplectic surface with Reeb-orbit boundary includes familiar transverse sections, but the theorem does not require globality.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only says the allowed surface could be a global surface of section, without analyzing such a case

passes.reason: It is cited only as a standard permitted kind of transverse surface, not as a special structural or failing case.

examples.name: Irrational ellipsoid with a p,q cylinder
examples.note: The irrational ellipsoid example has only two simple Reeb orbits and a cylinder-like surface with no interior intersections, forcing the non-generic theorem to allow boundary-orbit frequencies and illustrating equality in the bound.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker defines the irrational ellipsoid, computes its Reeb vector field, orbits/actions, constructs the p,q cylinder, and checks the area-volume and boundary frequency equality

passes.reason: The irrational ellipsoid cylinder gives a sharp equality case with no interior-intersecting Reeb orbits, showing the stronger nongeneric statement fails.
the toric irrational ellipsoid with the p,q cylinder is a highly regular sharp case with only boundary Reeb orbits, used to show the stronger interior-intersection/generic statement can fail
The irrational ellipsoid with the p,q cylinder gives a non-generic case with no interior-intersecting Reeb orbits and attains the theorem's frequency bound by equality on the boundary orbits.
The irrational ellipsoid/cylinder gives a sharp equality case with no interior periodic intersections, used to rule out a stronger non-generic interior-intersection claim.
the irrational-ellipsoid cylinder has no interior-intersecting Reeb orbits and saturates the stated area-volume bound on the boundary.

The irrational ellipsoid with the p,q cylinder has no interior-intersecting Reeb orbits, blocking a stronger non-generic existence claim, while its boundary orbits attain the area/volume bound exactly.

The irrational ellipsoid with the p,q cylinder shows that non-generic interior-intersection/equidistribution statements can fail, while the boundary orbits attain the theorem’s frequency bound with equality.
The irrational ellipsoid with the p,q cylinder is used to show non-generic interior-intersection/equidistribution expectations fail, and it is a tight equality case with minimal Reeb dynamics.
the irrational ellipsoid with the p,q cylinder has no interior-intersecting Reeb orbits and attains the boundary equality, blocking a stronger non-generic interior-existence statement.
The toric irrational ellipsoid with the p,q cylinder gives a highly regular sharp equality case with no interior-intersecting Reeb orbits, refuting a naive nongeneric interior-orbit conclusion.
Used to show the nongeneric interior-intersection/equidistribution conclusion can fail, while the toric irrational ellipsoid with the cylinder gives a sharp equality/minimal-Reeb-orbit model.
The irrational ellipsoid cylinder shows the nongeneric failure of interior intersecting periodic Reeb orbits and gives a tight equality case for the theorem.

examples.name: Rigid rotation of the disk
examples.note: For a disk map that is rotation by angle 2πθ0 everywhere, the Calabi invariant is simply θ0, making the action-function normalization concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the rigid disk rotation is only used as a quick example for the Calabi invariant/rotation-number interpretation, with no derivation shown

passes.reason: A rigid disk rotation is the rotationally symmetric model used only to interpret the Calabi invariant.

examples.name: Frequency sets consisting of rationals in an interval
examples.note: In the closing discussion, the speaker notes examples where the set of Reeb-surface frequencies is not itself an interval, such as rational values in an interval, to clarify the open question about closures.
examples.engagement_reason: frequency sets like rational numbers in an interval are invoked as possible examples when asking about closures, without constructing or analyzing them
